coredata.dc:title: Simple one step synthesis of dual-emissive heteroatom doped carbon dots for acetone sensing in commercial products and Cr (VI) reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Bogireddy, Naveen Kumar Reddy
Sotelo Rios, Sergio Eduardo
Agarwal, Vivechana
coredata.dc:description: A simple, sustainable and cost-effective fabrication technique for obtaining stable and multifunctional nanoprobes has been a challenging task for the scientific community. In this report, we optimized the fabrication conditions to obtain dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs) using simple one-step synthesis. The optimization of the actual conjugation length, dopant concentration, and the amount of surface functional groups could be seen in the form of intriguing characteristics of DECDs. Apart from being highly stable under continuous UV excitation, extensive range of pH, wide temperature range and saline solution, the proposed nanoprobes revealed efficient fluorescence in the complete visible range at a certain excitation wavelength, which was found to be favorable for selective ratiometric optical sensing of acetone, Cr(VI) and Fe(III). The sensing performance of carbon dots (CDs) for acetone was additionally verified in commercial products and spiked urine samples. Although the sensitivity of the probes for Cr (VI) and Fe (III) in spiked tap water samples was found to be comparable with the available techniques, the added advantage of the label-free reduction of highly toxic Cr(VI) to relatively less toxic Cr(III), makes the proposed nanomaterial more viable and effective for contaminated/waste water treatment. The proposed scalable strategies have potential applications in water purification and the possible development of economically viable portable ketone testing kits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-emission
Optical sensing
Acetone
Chromium and iron

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability tests for dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
Selectivity and sensitivity procedure for acetone sensing
Selectivity and sensitivity procedure for chromium (VI) and iron (III) sensing
Possible understanding for the generation of dual-emissive CDs
Stability tests for synthesized dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
Possible mechanism for the detection of acetone in the presence of dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
Chromium (VI) and iron (III) sensing using DECDs
Possible mechanism for Cr (VI) and Fe (III) sensing using dual-emissive carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: VA acknowledges the financial grant from CONACyT Basic Sciences project A1-S-30393. We acknowledge the help provided by Dr. Valenzuela from IPN-Mexico (TEM and XPS facility).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Florescence studies of CDs (a) at different dopant concentrations (N, 2 N, 3 N and 4 N) under the excitation of 250 nm (b) different microwave reaction times (35–70 s) λex = 254 nm (dopant concentration N) and (c) λex = 350 nm at dopant concentration N, (d) the optimized dual-emissive carbon dots (i.e., dopant concentration N and microwave reaction time 40 s) excited under 250–260 nm (inset shows the digital image of DECDs, under λex = 254 nm) and (e) excited with wavelengths in the range 310–500 nm (inset shows the corresponding excitation vs emission wavelengths), and (f) represents the Commission Internationale de ĹEclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity coordinates of N, 2 N, 3 N and 4 N, are (0.21,0.26), (0.18, 0.19), (0.19, 0.21) and (0.25, 0.39) under the excitation of 250 nm (left side), and similarly DECDs at different reaction times from 35 to 70 s under the excitation of 254 nm (right side).

Fluorescence studies of 2 N-CDs with excitation wavelength of (a) 230 to 300 nm, (b) 310 to 400 nm, 3 N-CDs with excitation wavelength of (c) 230 to 300 nm, (d) 310 to 410 nm, 4 N-CDs with excitation wavelength of (e) 230 to 300 nm, and (f) 310 to 470 nm. All the CDs were fabricated with 40 s of reaction time.

The optical properties of DECDs using (a) UV–visible absorbance, PL emission, and excitation spectra at different emission wavelengths and (b) Normalized UV–visible absorbance studies of CDs synthesized with N to 4 N urea concentrations at 40 s, (c) Concentration (6–20 μL in 405 μL total volume) dependent PL emission of DECDs under 254 nm excitation (inset shows the normalized PL intensity spectra and their corresponding digital photographs and (d) the corresponding Commission Internationale de ĹÉclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity coordinates.

Basic morphological and structural characterization of the as-prepared DECDs with 1:1 wt concentration of CA: urea at 40 s microwave reaction time (a) TEM, (b, d) HRTEM, (c) particle size distribution studies, (e) FFT image, (f) XRD patterns, (g) FTIR and (h) normalized PL spectra at different excitations (254, 310, and 420 nm) of the as-prepared DECDs.

Structural characterization of synthesized CDs through XPS. The deconvoluted C1s, N1s and O1s from high resolution XPS scan measured for CDs prepared with different citric acid: Urea weight ratio (a-c) N, (d-f) 2 N and (g-i) 4 N.

Selectivity of dual-emissive carbon dots towards acetone, tested using PL spectroscopy (a) PL response (at λex = 254 nm) of DECDs measured with different solvents (5 μL DECDs stock solution into 400 μL of solvent (99.9%)) concentration; except DMSO (600 Volts) PL signal for all other solvents was measured under PMT detector voltage of 900 Volts, (b) PL bar graph representing the response of optimized CDs (DECDs) at λem = 445 and 500 nm, (λex = 254 nm) for 7 different solvents. Annihilation of DECDs’ PL signal intensity in different concentrations of (c) Acetone, (d) commercial product with acetone in DD water, (e) Presence of commercial product with acetone in urine, and their corresponding (f) PL intensity (at 507 nm; taken from (c), (d) and (e)) vs acetone concentration graphs revealing linear fit with R2 values corresponding to pure acetone spiked DD water, commercial product with acetone present in DD water and commercial product (with acetone) spiked in urine as 0.9839, 0.9682, and 0.9690 (at λem ≈ 507 nm) respectively).

Selectivity of dual-emissive carbon dots towards Cr (VI) and Fe (III) using PL spectroscopy (a) normalized PL relative intensities (F1/F0) at λem = 447 nm, λex = 254 nm, (normalization done with respect to as-prepared DECDs PL intensity maxima in deionized water (F0); F1 represents PL intensity of DECDs in the presence of metal ions); Annihilation of PL signal intensity of aq. DECDs in the presence of (b) different concentrations of Cr (VI) (c) Analysis graph representing PLmax intensity variation for different Cr(VI) concentrations revealing the linear fit (d) Aq. DECDs PL signal annihilation in the presence of Fe (III) ions, (e) PL intensity vs concentration graphs corresponding to the data presented in (d) the linear fit ; with R2 values corresponding to Cr (VI) and Fe (III) as 0.9947 and 0.9773 at λem = 447 nm, along with 0.9974 and 0.9246 at λem ≈ 507 nm. Their digital photographs taken at different metal ion concentrations in day light and two different UV excitation wavelengths (f) Cr (VI) and (g) Fe (III).

Structural (XPS) characterization of synthesized DECDs in presence of Cr (VI). The high‐resolution (a) O1s, (b) Cr2p, (c) C1s and (d) N1s spectrum respectively. The survey scan is given in SI (Figure S11) (dotted line in Fig. 8b represents original data).

General and field studies considered for the world health organization (WHO) and Biologischer arbeitsstoff-toleranz-Wert (BAT) values of (a) acetone, (b) chromium (Cr (VI)), and (c) Iron (Fe (III)) [15–18].
Schematic drawing of the proposed mechanism: fabrication of dual-emissive CDs by controlling carbonizing degree (reaction time) using sustainable sources.
Schematic illustration of the proposed quenching mechanism: Cr (VI)/Fe (III) (left side of the schematic) and acetone detection (right side of the schematic).

head.ce:title: Simple one step synthesis of dual-emissive heteroatom doped carbon dots for acetone sensing in commercial products and Cr (VI) reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas, UAEM, Av. Univ. 1001, Col. Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos 62209, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas, UAEM, Av. Univ. 1001, Col. Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos 62209, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, sustainable and cost-effective fabrication technique for obtaining stable and multifunctional nanoprobes has been a challenging task for the scientific community. In this report, we optimized the fabrication conditions to obtain dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs) using simple one-step synthesis. The optimization of the actual conjugation length, dopant concentration, and the amount of surface functional groups could be seen in the form of intriguing characteristics of DECDs. Apart from being highly stable under continuous UV excitation, extensive range of pH, wide temperature range and saline solution, the proposed nanoprobes revealed efficient fluorescence in the complete visible range at a certain excitation wavelength, which was found to be favorable for selective ratiometric optical sensing of acetone, Cr(VI) and Fe(III). The sensing performance of carbon dots (CDs) for acetone was additionally verified in commercial products and spiked urine samples. Although the sensitivity of the probes for Cr (VI) and Fe (III) in spiked tap water samples was found to be comparable with the available techniques, the added advantage of the label-free reduction of highly toxic Cr(VI) to relatively less toxic Cr(III), makes the proposed nanomaterial more viable and effective for contaminated/waste water treatment. The proposed scalable strategies have potential applications in water purification and the possible development of economically viable portable ketone testing kits.

ce:section.ce:para: Dual/multiple-emissive (D/ME) fluorescent particles have gained considerable interest due to their possible application as ratiometric optical sensors [1]. Most of the white-light emitters (WLE@CDs) reported so far, rely on the merging of three primary RGB (red, green and blue) colors. Till now, several methods have been adopted to develop efficient WLE structures using D/ME, which include the usage of materials such as carbon quantum dots [2], polymers [3], nanoparticles [4], small molecules [5], and metal–organic framework [6]. Unlike CDs with multiple emission, a single-molecule WLE has been shown to have various compensations such as enhanced stability, ease in production process, and reproducibility [7–11].

Recently, some research groups [12,13] used organically derived source and rare earth metal ions to fabricate D/MECDs. Chen et al. [12] prepared the terbium ion doped DECDs demonstrating a single peak in the absence of analyte; nevertheless, the energy transfer from the analyte to terbium ion through non-radiative transition, successfully resulted in antenna effect giving rise to dual-emission. Similarly, green fluorescent dye, basic fuchsin and citric acid were recently used by Jin [13] group (as sources of carbon) to fabricate DECDs for optical pH sensing.

Among the fluorescence-based sensing materials, although carbon based fluorescent materials are being highlighted in environmental (as optical sensor) applications due to their efficient and tunable PL properties, biocompatibility, and high photostability in visible region, not many studies have been carried out to test their stability under continuous UV exposure, thermal treatment, different pH, and in NaCl solution. Moreover, the surrounding organic solvent medium, in which CDs are suspended, play a major role in the system application part [14].

On the other hand, among the several organic solvents, acetone (ketone) is one of the major products found in plants, forest fires, volcanic gases and tobacco smoke. It is also produced by several industries and used in house hold products (paint remover and nail polish remover) as well as filter change [15]. In real life, the ketones are waste by-products created in our body while burning fat (instead of glucose). Based on several studies (presented in Scheme 1 ), the corresponding threshold values in excreted urine are 40 ppm acetone [16]. Among the heavy metals, Chromium (VI) is one the major contaminant in the environment, based on health concerns such as carcinogenicity and genotoxicity. As a practical measure, International Standards (WHO) have been set for recommended limits of concentration of 1 μM [17]. In addition, it is necessary to detect Fe (III) due to its availability and their vital role in human nutrition [18]. The Fe (III) is present in ground waters (without any filtration or discoloration) up to several milligrams per liter. Although it is difficult to recognize Fe (III) by tasting concentrations less than 0.3 ppm, color and turbidity can be identified in piped house systems at 0.05–0.1 ppm. Moreover, sanitary and laundry ware will stain at concentrations higher than 0.3 ppm. According to the WHO estimates, the maximum concentration limit of iron in drinking water is 0.3 mg/l.

In the present work, we established a simple strategy to fabricate dual--emissive nitrogen doped carbon dots (DECDs), by controlling the synthesis parameters using the microwave technique. The optical properties of DECDs were optimized by testing different parameters related to the precursors/reactants (citric acid, urea) and reaction time. By increasing the microwave reaction time and dopant concentration, the maximum PL emission of the resulting DECDs is found to gradually shift from blue to green (in electromagnetic spectral region). Interestingly, through controlled graphitization, the PL emission maxima of DECDs can be modulated from 430 to 512 nm. As per our knowledge, this is a novel strategy that can be used for the fabrication of very efficient water-soluble multiple-emissive DECDs with simple one-step microwave technique, using sustainable precursors like citric acid and urea. The stability of as-prepared DECDs was tested under continuous UV excitation, large pH range, different temperatures and saline solution concentration. The application of the proposed DECD probes has been demonstrated as optical acetone detector in urine and a typical commercial product along with, heavy metal (Cr (VI) and Fe (III)) detection and sensing. The detection of highly toxic Cr(VI) was found to be accompanied by its reduction to relatively less toxic Cr(III).

Citric acid (CA), Urea, K2CO3, KOH, Na2SO4, Na2HCO3, NaBr, NaCN, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, PbCl2, MnCl2, NaNO3, SnCl2, FeCl3, CuCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, CuCl, HgCl2, ZnCl2 and K2Cr2O7, phosphate buffers were procured from Sigma Aldrich and utilized without any prior treatment. Double distilled water (DD water) was used throughout the experiments. Commercial products were acquired from local supermarket for sensing applications.

Dual-emissive CDs were fabricated using citric acid and urea in double distilled water (DD water) with different weight ratios (range of 0.5 – 4.0) of citric acid and urea. According to the weight ratio of CA: urea, they were denoted as 0.5 N, N, 2 N, 3 N and 4 N corresponding to 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3 and 1:4 respectively. The microwave power was maintained throughout the experiments at 280 W and reaction time was controlled from 35 to 70 s to control the extent of graphitization. The typical synthesis process in brief can be explained as follows: 0.25 g citric acid and 0.25 g urea (1:1 wt ratio) were dissolved in 3 mL distilled water and stirred for 10 min to make a clear solution, followed by microwave heating treatment for 40 s for the formation of dark yellow semi-solid precipitate (i.e., DECDs). The formed product was kept under ambient conditions for 30 min, followed by its dissolution in 2 mL of DD water under constant stirring for 20 min. The final product was centrifuged several times in water/ethanol before storing them at RT for further use. The characterization specifications are presented in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability tests for dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
ce:section.ce:para: As a part of stability test, initially the photostability of as-prepared dual-emissive CDs (DECDs) was tested under continuous excitation (λex = 254 nm ; λem = 440 and 510 nm) for a time period of 800 s using PL Spectrophotometer. Furthermore, to identify the stability in acidic and basic media, a 5 μL volume of DECDs were dispersed in 400 μL volume of phosphate buffer (pH studies). Similarly, the concentration (concentration of DECDs) dependent salinity test (PL signal stability in different concentrations of NaCl solution), temperature and dosage studies were also measured using PL spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity procedure for acetone sensing
ce:section.ce:para: For relative selectivity determination of DECDs towards acetone, florescence studies were conducted with some common organic solvents in the presence of sensing probe (5 µL of DECDs stock solution in 400 µL of organic solvent solution) [19]. The corresponding PL signal response was examined under identical conditions of DECDs solution with different solvents such as ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), Isopropanol (Isop), DMSO, DMF and Acetone. Among them, acetone shows complete quenching in the PL signal intensity with increasing concentrations from 1.55 to 155 ppm respectively. After constant agitation for 20 s, PL spectra were recorded (λex = 254 nm) in the range of 340 – 700 nm under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity procedure for chromium (VI) and iron (III) sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the relative selectivity of DECDs towards Cr (VI) and Fe (III), optical sensing measurements were carried out on some heavy metal ions in a certain volumetric proportion of 1:80, i.e., 5 µL of DECDs stock solution in 400 µL of 1 mM metal solution. The optical spectra of DECDs were inspected using 1 μL of formed DECDs’ stock solution with 1 mM K2CO3, KOH, Na2SO4, Na2HCO3, NaBr, NaCN, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, PbCl2, MnCl2, NaNO3, SnCl2, FeCl3, CuCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, CuCl, HgCl2, ZnCl2 and K2Cr2O7. After constant stirring for 0.5 min under ambient conditions, PL spectra were measured using Cary Eclipse Varian PL spectroscopy (λex = 254 nm) in range of 360 – 700 nm.

To know the effect of combination parameters on the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the as-prepared carbon dots, initially five typical CDs were prepared by changing the concentration of urea with respect to citric acid. As mentioned earlier in the experimental section, (according to the weight ratio of CA: urea) the samples 0.5 N, N, 2 N, 3 N and 4 N corresponding to 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3 and 1:4 respectively, were fabricated at the same microwave power (280 W) and time (40 s). Fig. 1 and S1 (ESI), shows the PL (measured using Cary Eclipse spectrometer) characteristics of carbon dots as a function of heteroatom dopant concentration (urea) in aqueous medium under different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 1a shows an abrupt change between 1:3 to 1:4 (citric acid: urea ratio; 3 N to 4 N) revealed in the form of high PL intensity of CDs changed from blue to green light emission region (445 nm to 520 nm at 254 nm excitation). This indicated that higher weight ratio of urea in CA leads to the red shift in the PL emission of CDs. Furthermore, CDs (type N) were also fabricated as a function of microwave reaction time (i.e., part of optimization of dual-emissive CDs with similar PL signal intensities) (Fig. 1b, c) and the corresponding PL measurements are presented in Fig. 1b, c under 254 and 350 nm excitation wavelengths. With an increase in reaction time from > 35 s, the CDs display green emission with a red shift with respect to the initial reaction time (35 s) at 1:1 (N) weight ratio of CA: urea. As higher reaction time will promote the graphitization of CA [20,21], we understand that the PL characteristics of CDs highly depend on the reaction time. Moreover, dual-emission with uneven (i.e., ascendency of one emission over the other emission) signal intensities of CDs was observed at 1:1 concentration ratio (Sample N; Fig. 1d). From Fig. 1d (corresponding to CDs fabricated at N concentration), we observed dual PL emissive peaks around 443 and 513 nm (i.e., bluish white emission) with 254 nm excitation wavelength. When the CDs are excited with any wavelength between 250 and 260 nm, we can clearly observe the dominance of one emission over the other emission peak, in terms of PL intensity. Additionally, when the as-prepared CDs with dual-emission (named as dual-emissive CDs (DECDs)) are excited with 250 to 350 nm, the broad emission spectrum, dominated by dual-emissive peaks (around 445 and 510 nm) is observed, which in turn depends on excitation wavelength within the measured interval. Similar excitation wavelength dependent emission is observed, when excited with any wavelength between 360 and 510 nm (Fig. 1e). Hence, the DECDs exhibit both excitation dependent and independent emission properties (depending on the excitation wavelength range), and minimal quantity of green emissive CDs (i.e., strong PL emission ≈514 nm) can be identified at higher excitation wavelengths (>400 nm).

The change in PL spectra of CDs on changing reaction parameters and environments has been demonstrated via CIE coordinates in Fig. 1f, i.e. color coordinates (x, y) corresponding to N, 2 N, 3 N and 4 N at 254 nm excitation wavelength are (0.21,0.26), (0.18, 0.19), (0.19, 0.21) and (0.25, 0.39) respectively. Among them, N-CDs exhibited a bluish white emission as compared with other three concentrations, whereas the PL emission signal intensity can be further improved by tuning their carbonization degree (microwave reaction time).

Furthermore, in the case of CDs at 1:2 (2 N) weight ratio, the major emission comes from ≈ 437 nm with the excitation wavelength range from 230 to 400 nm (Fig. 2 (a, b)), but interestingly we can observe dual-emissive peaks around 437 and 520 nm when excited with wavelengths ranging from 260 to 290 nm. Although observable, the shift from N to 2 N weight ratios of CDs is relatively weak in terms of emission intensity with respect to DECDs.

For 1:3 (3 N), CDs show similar behavior like 2 N CDs and in addition a small red shift from 437 to 442 nm and 520 to 523 nm in emission peak wavelength (Fig. 2 (c, d)). More information corresponding 3 N CDs and 4 N CDs is presented in Fig. 2 (e, f).

Similarly, for 1:4 (4 N) CDs, dual-emissive peaks are revealed around 447 and 523 nm, when excited with wavelengths in the range 250–290 nm. Although similar spectra with two emission wavelengths (≈450 and ≈522 nm) at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 360 nm and 370 to 470 nm were observed, drastic change in the emission peak wavelength from ≈ 450 to ≈ 522 nm was observed in the excitation wavelength range 360–380 nm, which was accompanied with dual-emission at 370 nm excitation. Regardless of excitation wavelength, the existence of two emission wavelengths indicates that 4 N-CDs have excitation independent emissions in a big range of wavelengths (Fig. 2 (e, f)). These results suggest that under certain reaction conditions, the emission properties of CDs prepared by microwave method can be customized similar to pyrolysis technique [21].

Fig. 3 a shows the typical optical properties of DECDs, i.e, absorbance, PL emission (excitation wavelength 254 nm), and PL excitation spectra at 3 different emission wavelengths. DECDs show absorption band at around 340 nm with an observable tail peak up to 730 nm (Fig. 3b), which is normally attributed to n to π* transitions corresponding to conjugated C = O and C = N [20]. As mentioned earlier, the PL emission of DECDs, centered at ≈ 445 nm and ≈ 510 nm (with the excitation wavelength of 254 nm), closely matches with the PLE spectrum at ≈ 310 nm, ≈ 445 nm, and ≈ 510 nm and the corresponding absorption band at ≈220 to 340 nm, indicates that n-π* transitions from conjugated C = N and C = O are accountable for the emission [20]. Similar tendencies were observed for the rest of the CDs (2 N, 3 N and 4 N) as well. The obtained PL signal is majorly the contribution from the absorbance spectrum corresponding to the aromatic sp2 arrangement [20].

The effect of CDs’ concentration was tested by preparing 6–20 µL of DECDs (N; 1:1) into 600 µL solution with DD water (Fig. 1e). It can be noticed that the PL emission of DECDs steadily rises up to 16 µL/600 µL, with an increase in CDs’ concentration in the solution. Any further increase results in the decrease of the PL intensity (Fig. 3c). Such behavior indicates that the as-synthesized CDs show the self-quenching mechanism, with a substantial change in the PL signal (bluish white emission to complete green emission), and the corresponding color coordinates are presented in the Fig. 3d.

Fig. 4 (a, b) demonstrates the transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM-ARM200F) images corresponding to DECDs synthesized with N weight ratio at 40 s of microwave reaction time. The dual-emissive CDs possess majorly two different average particle sizes, which are 3.1 ± 0.2 and 4.0 ± 0.1 nm (Fig. 4c). The FFT analysis from HR-TEM images illustrate that the DECDs have highly crystalline carbon structure (Fig. 4 (d, e)). In conformity with the previous studies, two types of lattice fringe distances measured in the present case (0.24 and 0.34 nm), have been assigned to the d-spacings of the graphene (100) and graphite (001) planes [20,21].

Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer) pattern presented in Fig. 4f, exhibits a broad and a minor sharp peak at 21.78° and 29.63°, corresponding to the graphitic carbon structure with respective d-spacing of 4.1 and 3.1 Å. The broad pattern with an enlarged inter layer distance is possibly due to the distortion from sp3 C in the plane [21]. The peak at 29.63° is assigned to the characteristic (002) plane [21], where its presence indicates that the interlayer stacking within the as-prepared DECDs is close to that of graphite (≈3.5 Å). It shows that the constricted packing of sp2 domain takes vital place within DECDs.

To further understand the role of functional groups, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of as-prepared DECDs is presented in Fig. 4g, the stretching vibrations for C = O (1721 cm−1), C = N (1667 cm−1), C = C (1635 cm−1), CH2 (2936 cm−1), NH (3230 cm−1), and OH (3437 cm−1) reveal the existence of conjugated aromatic structure composed of N- and O- containing groups. Among these stretching vibrations, C = C, C = N and C = O were found to be increasing with increasing dopant ratio (Figure S2, ESI) [20].

Furthermore, to investigate the composition of heteroatom doped carbon dots with respect to dopant weight ratio, XPS was employed. Figure S3 shows the XPS survey scan of the synthesized CDs (N, 2 N and 4 N), which were collected for Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), and Nitrogen (N) species. The relative intensity ratio of nitrogen to carbon (N1s to C1s) varies from 0.244 (Figure S3a), 0.319 (Figure S3b) to 0.848 (Figure S3c), representing an increase in the nitrogen content, with increasing urea content during the synthesis. Similarly, oxygen to carbon intensity ratio increases from 0.555 (Figure S3a), 0.784 (Figure S3b) to 1.308 (Figure S3c), with increasing dopant weight ratio of N, 2 N and 4 N respectively. This indicates that an increase in oxygen content, closely matches with FTIR analysis presented in the Fig. 4g and Figure S2 (ESI). Fig. 5 (a, d and g) illustrates the high resolution C1s spectra of CDs as function of dopant concentration i.e., N, 2 N and 4 N respectively. The C1s spectra can be convoluted into 4 categories of C species such as –COOH, -C=O, sp3 C (C-O and C-N) and sp2 C(C = C/C–C) corresponding to 289.5, 287.8, 285.6 and 284.3 eV, respectively [20]. It demonstrates that the content of all C species groups significantly increases from N to 4 N, which closely matches with FTIR results. The deconvoluted O1s spectra (Fig. 5; 2nd column) contains two groups, i.e., C = O and C-O at 531.8 and 534.1 eV [22] respectively. The N1s spectra (3rd column; Fig. 5) has been deconvoluted into graphitic N/ amine N and pyridinic N at 400.3 and 399.3 eV, respectively. The relative content ratio of graphitic N to pyridinic N changes from 0.420, 0.645 to 0.783 with an increasing dopant concentration in sample N, 2 N and 4 N respectively, indicating an increase in the graphitic N content with increasing weight ratio of urea in CA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible understanding for the generation of dual-emissive CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been shown theoretically that the dimension of the quantum dots is given by the effective graphitization or equivalently its effective conjugation length [21]. In addition, taking into account the theoretical calculations from Xiong group [23] on the size dependent fluorescence (PL) emission wavelength of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the emission wavelength tunability from deep UV to NIR and its linear dependence with the GQDs’ dimensions have been demonstrated. This change in the emission wavelength has been associated to the corresponding changes in the bandgap [21]. In accordance with the above-mentioned studies, dual-emission from the CDs (synthesized at 1:1 wt ratios of CA: urea@40 s) in the present case, can be attributed to the formation of two-dominating sizes i.e., 3.1 ± 0.2 and 4.0 ± 0.1 nm confirmed through TEM analysis (refer Fig. 4c). From this information, it can be assumed that under certain synthesis conditions the occurrence of higher extent of graphitization (4 N CDs; an increase in the microwave reaction time in the present case) results in enlarged effective conjugation length (sp2 domain) and hence emission wavelength shifts from blue to green. In addition, another study reports [21,23] that the addition of both –OH and –COOH further shifts the emission from green to red. In conformity with the above-mentioned reports, along with the FTIR and XPS analysis in the present case (refer to Fig. 3, S2 and Fig. 5), confirmed an increasing amount of –COOH groups from lower to higher (N to 4 N) concentration of dopant. This increase in –COOH groups in CDs can be an additional factor resulting in the red shift of the emission wavelength. On the basis of the above results, it can be concluded that the red shift of emission wavelength from CDs, with an increase in dopant concentration, is the joint contribution from both the enhanced extent of graphitization and increased amount of –COOH groups on the surface in the CDs (Scheme 2 ). In addition, it should be noticed that the UV absorbance spectrum of 0.5 N-CDs (Fig. 3b) shows only one absorption peak at 340 nm (no absorption peak in visible region is observed) as compared to N to 4 N-CDs with dual absorption peaks (one in UV and the other in the visible region). In addition, the photoluminescence spectra corresponding to lower urea concentration (Fig. S1; 0.5 N-CDs) reveals the formation of CDs having the emission of only blue light. Similarly, C1s band in XPS deconvoluted into -C = O, sp3 C (C-O and C-N) and sp2 C(C = C/C–C), and –COOH in N-CDs (Fig. 5a), indicate that the green emissions of N to 4 N-CDs should originate from the nitrogen dopant concentration.Scheme 3.

Hence, there are 3 possible developments involved in the formation of dual-emissive carbon dots with citric acid and urea as precursors, i.e., the size distribution, enhancement in the formation of –COOH groups and the defects induced by the dopants which in turn result in the availability of different molecular and surface states.

On the basis of the above analysis, N-CDs (named as DECDs) were taken as optimized nanoprobes for dual-emission and were used to study the stability and their potential application as optical sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability tests for synthesized dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Figure S4a (ESI), reveals there is no observable change in the PL signal intensity after 800 min of continuous excitation (with 254 nm) under Xenon flash lamp. The measurements were taken for the emission wavelengths of 440 and 510 nm with an average acquisition time of 0.1 s for each measurement. The proposed nanoprobes were observed to be relatively stable with respect to the already reported literature. As an example, Lesani et al. [20], reported multicolor fluorescent CDs, synthesized using ascorbic acid and L-arginine as precursors, which show around 10% PL signal decrement within 30 min. Similarly, in our recently published work [19], fluorescent nitrogen doped oxidized CDs, prepared via hydrothermal heat treatment of citric acid and urea, exhibited around 6% decrement in emission in 400 min under continuous excitation (λex = 254 nm) with pulsed Xe lamp. Thus, the proposed as-prepared dual-emissive carbon dots show higher photostability than previously reported CDs [19,20].

The as-prepared dual-emissive carbon dots show an excellent solubility and no precipitation after the addition of different concentrations of NaCl. The emission response stability of DECDs was monitored for 0.0–2.0 M NaCl concentration range. A noteworthy alteration in the signal intensity was observed at high concentrations (>1.0 M) of salt (Figure S4b, ESI). The overall outcome elucidates that the DECDs revealed good dispersibility and promising optical properties in NaCl salt solution.

Apart from the continuous excitation and NaCl tests, pH and temperature dependent fluorescence behavior also plays a major role from the application point of view [24]. A complete quenching of PL intensity of as-prepared DECDs is observed in acidic medium (pH = 2 to 4). Although, with pH tuning from 6 to 8 (Figure S5a) no observable change in the PL signal intensity (compared with neutral pH) can be seen, at pH 10, a drastic change in the PL peak intensity is observed in the form of an observable shift of around ≈15 nm (with respect to 445 nm). As compared with other pHs, the shift at pH 10 is accompanied with an enhanced PL intensity and a change from broad emission to narrow emission. Figure S5a shows the digital images corresponding to the 5 pHs in the range from 2 to 10 (viewed under 254 nm wavelength of the UV lamp) and the corresponding CIE color coordinates are presented in the Figure S5b.

Moreover, the complete quenching of PL signal intensity in the pH range 2 to 4, could be due to the aggregation (higher concentration of hydrogen ions may participate in the reaction with –COOH groups present in the CDs) of DECDs. Interestingly, at pH = 10, only one size of CDs (with emission ≈ 440 nm) are participating in the reaction and enhancing the signal intensity due to the large availability of –OH groups which may react with the –COOH group present in the DECDs. This pH effect reveals the importance of basic media cites (–OH) for the blue and green emission from the fluorescent species in the DECDs. As the fluorescence is quenched in the acidic media, we understand that a particular size of CDs is restored only in the basic medium [25]. Such significant changes in the PL intensity with pH can be explored in future studies to detect compounds with basic nature.

Additionally, the temperature dependent PL studies were carried out (Figure S5c) at 19 different temperatures in the given range (5 °C −90 °C). There is a slight decrease in the PL signal intensity at higher temperatures (70 °C- 90 °C) (Figure S5d). The CDs show a slight domination in the green region peak with respect to blue region peak. Apart from that there is no major effect of temperature (in 5–70 °C range) on CDs, which demonstrates the stability of CDs at high temperature in aqueous medium. These stability studies make the proposed DECDs extremely useful for designing commercially viable applications [26].

Keeping in view the relative optical stability of dual-emissive carbon dots, they were tested for the possible development of optical sensing application. Primarily, the experiments were carried out in different organic solvents due to their importance in industries and household materials (as mentioned in introduction; section 1). Moreover, as the surrounding medium affects the optical properties of CDs, the proposed nanoprobes were dispersed in several organic solvents such as Ethanol (EtOH), Methanol (MeOH), Isopropanol (Iso), N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and Acetone, and their corresponding optical properties were measured using PL spectroscopy. The fluorescence spectra of DECDs, dispersed in different organic solvents, exhibit distinct emission wavelengths and PL signal intensities (Fig. 6 (a)).

Fig. 6(a, b) shows the PL emission of CDs (at λex = 254 nm) in different solvents and the analysis/tendency of dual-emission intensity maximas (λem = 445 and 500 nm). In the presence of DMSO, EtOH, MeOH and isopropanol, dual-emissive carbon dots show an enhancement in the PL signal intensity at λem ≈ 500 nm and the changes in PL peak emission wavelength are also observed (PL detector voltage was kept at 900 V for all solvents except DMSO with 600 V). The presence of DMF and acetone in the CDs is found to quench the PL signal. Furthermore, the sensitivity towards acetone detection in DD water, was carried out by adding the optimized nanoprobes in different concentrations of acetone (1.55 to 155 ppm). The PL signal intensity is found to continuously decrease with an increase in acetone concentration. Such sensitivity studies, when extended to acetone present in the commercial product (NP remover in the present work) and urine with commercial product (Fig. 6 (c-f) and Figure S6), revealed almost similar tendencies. A small variation in the presence of urine sample, is attributed to the presence of other moieties in the urine (i.e., heavy metals, colorants, etc.) [27]. The detection limit calculated for acetone spiked DD water, commercial product in DD water and urine samples was 1.07, 1.02 and 0.40 ppm respectively. The exponential fit of DECDs in acetone and DECDs with nail polish remover, with corresponding regression factor of 0.9979 and 0.9962 respectively, is shown in Figure S7. The obtained results confirm the potential application of DECDs as sensing platforms for the recognition acetone in actual commercial products and urine samples. In addition, we present additional sensitivity studies corresponding to all alcohols used in the work (Figure S8). From this we can conclude that although the proposed sensing probes are sensitive to other analytes, the phenomenon is revealed only at very high concentrations, which is far more than the minimum toxicity levels laid down by world health organization. Hence, the proposed nanoprobes have been contemplated as sensors only for acetone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the detection of acetone in the presence of dual-emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence signal intensity has been reported [27–29] to get influenced by the properties of the organic medium such as viscosity, refractive index/dielectric constant, solvatochromic shifts, polarizability, hydrogen bonding, along with accepting and donating abilities of solvent. However, in the present study, the PL signal intensities and emission wavelengths of DECDs responds anonymously in the presence of organic solvents, which means that no particular dependence of the signal intensity and emission wavelength on the above-mentioned physical parameters of the organic solvents was observed. Our PL signal trends are similar to the published literature from Das et al., [27]. Moreover, in the previous reports, by the addition of organic solvents to CDs, the solvent medium could immediately passivate the surface of CDs [27], and hence several other factors (such as polarity, steric effect, etc) need to be considered for establishing a possible detection mechanism [27].

In the present case, the PL signal quenching is attributed to the electron transfer (donating or accepting) ability of the quencher molecule. The XPS and FTIR studies revealed the presence of several functional groups on the surface of CDs, e.g., amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups (ref to Fig. 4g, S1 and Fig. 5). In the presence of DMSO gives very high PL signal with respect to other solvents possibly due to the interaction between electron donating character of nitrogen/oxygen atoms in dual-emissive carbon dots with electron deficient sulphur atom in DMSO. Similarly, when a strong electron acceptor (hydroxyl groups) interacts with the surface states of sensing probe, the PL signal intensity is enhanced (in case of EtOH, MeOH and Isopropanol). In case of DMF and acetone, the fluorescence signal of the CDs is substantially less efficient. It is known that to stabilize the holes on the surface of the CDs, the electron donor species are required [27,29]. Being small in size and a good electron donor, acetone can easily coordinate with the ligands (hydroxyl and carboxyl) and quench the PL signal of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromium (VI) and iron (III) sensing using DECDs
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of different Cr(VI)/Fe(III) concentrations to DECDs (Fig. 7 (a-c)), results in drastic changes in the optical response (fluorescence as Fig. 7b and d), possibly due to the electron donor character of oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) atoms present in DECDs [30]. This observation establishes DECDs as potential candidate for the Cr (VI)/Fe (III) sensor. For Cr(VI) and Fe(III), sensing response shows a good linear correlation with R2 as 0.9947 and 0.9773 at λem = 447 nm, and 0.9974 and 0.9256 at λem = 507 nm respectively. Furthermore, the calculated limit of detection for concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 μM, using the formula 3 × standard deviation (SD)/slope(m) [19], was 3.2 µM for Cr (IV) and 4.1 µM for Fe (III) at signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 7 (c, e)). The corresponding digital photographs, taken under daylight and UV lamp (with excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm), are presented in Fig. 7 (f, g). In addition to the laboratory conditions, the sensitivity of DECDs towards Cr (VI)/Fe (III) was tested in spiked tap water samples and is presented in supplementary information (Figure S9).

In order to quantitatively determine the concentration of Cr(VI) and Fe(III), one needs the calibration curves of the respective species in DI / real tap water with the known concentrations. The comparison of the unknown concentration with the calibration curve will give us a quantitative estimation of the metal ions. The possible quantification of Cr(VI), Fe(III) in real samples with calibration curves is presented in the supplementary information (Figure S9 (c, d)). Additionally, we present sensitivity studies of some more heavy metal ions (HgCl2, CuCl2 and AgNO3) used in the work (Figure S10). Similar to acetone sensing, the studies on other heavy metals (Hg+2, Cu+2 and Ag+1) reveal that although the proposed sensing probes are sensitive to other analytes, the phenomenon is revealed only at very high concentrations, which is far more than the minimum toxicity levels laid down by world health organization. Hence, the proposed nanoprobes have been contemplated as sensors only for Cr(VI) and Fe(III).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for Cr (VI) and Fe (III) sensing using dual-emissive carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the previous studies, the quenching of CDs’ fluorescence signal in the presence of Cr (VI) depends on inner filter effect, metal–ligand charge transfer, fluorescence resonance and electronic energy transfer [31,32]. In addition, Zheng et al. [31], reported both inner filter effect and static quenching effect-based detection of Cr (VI) (using CDs). Similar to the previously reported mechanisms [33], the inevitability of the selectivity has been attributed to the presence of vacant “d” orbitals in Cr (VI) and low-lying d-d transition states, which aids the fluorescence quenching of the DECDs. Similarly, Fe (III) has an outer electronic configuration of 4s2d5, which means that it has five half-filled d orbitals in the outer shell. Therefore, when these ions are added to the DECD solution, the excited electrons from the conduction band of CD are transferred to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe (III). However, in the present case, the PL signal quenching has been attributed to the coordination of the metal ions with different functional groups of DECDs, due to the electron donor character of oxygen and nitrogen atoms present as dopants in DECDs (confirmed by FTIR (Fig. 4g) and XPS (Fig. 5 a-c)). To confirm the above mentioned hypothesis, XPS study of DECDs was carried out in the presence of 1 mM Cr(VI) (Fig. 8
, and ESI Figure S11). There is no observable difference in the XPS spectra of C1s and N1s as compared to O1s spectra. From the deconvolution of O1s spectra (Fig. 8a), a drastic decrease in the intensity of C-O is observed (as compared to DECDs without Cr (VI)), which in turn could possibly favor the electron transfer from DECDs to Cr (VI). Fig. 8b confirmed the presence of more Cr (III) as compared with Cr (VI), which confirms the conversion of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) in the presence of DECDs [34].

An additional study, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (Figure S12) was performed to investigate the reduction process in presence of DECDs. The obtained results exhibited a decrease in the alcoholic groups after the exposure to Cr(VI), which is understood as their utilization for the conversion of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and in turn the alcoholic groups are converted to carboxylic groups. Hence the carbon dots containing alcoholic groups could convert Cr (VI) to Cr (III) in water. This is in conformity with the previous studies conducted by Rodrigues group [35]. In addition, it has been mentioned that other functional groups present in the DECDs play a vital role in adsorption of the Cr(VI) ions but are not involved in the conversion of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) [34,35].

Furthermore, Zheng et al. [31], showed turn OFF-ON sensor by converting Cr (VI) to Cr (III) in the presence of reducing agents (i.e., Cystine (Cys) and ascorbic acid (AA)). Interestingly, in our case the direct reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) is obtained using DECDs. No observable PL intensity change (Figure S13) with an addition of AA or Cys to DECDs (in the presence of Cr (VI)), confirms the absence of any reaction due to the presence of Cr (III) in the CDs solution (nonradiative electron transfer). As per our knowledge, due to electron transfer similar reduction is taking place in the case of Fe(III) (Fe(III) to Fe (II)).

In summary, dual-emissive nitrogen doped carbon dots have been successfully fabricated through a facile and scalable one-pot synthesis route. Unlike the previous reports, the as-prepared DECDs exhibit PL signal covering the entire visible spectrum with an almost flat band signal, when excited at 254 nm. The multiple electronic transitions of DECDs have been correlated with their chemical composition/structure. The optimization in the synthesis parameters of nitrogen doped carbon dots was performed to obtain high PL response with highly efficient and stable dual-emission. The optical response of the proposed nanoprobes was found to be stable in saline solution, 800 min of continuous UV excitation, pH variations and temperature (5 to 90 °C range). In addition, DECDs showed excellent selectivity towards acetone, Cr (VI) and Fe (III) among 28 different analytes /heavy metals. Apart from the selectivity and high sensitivity under laboratory conditions, DECDs revealed outstanding acetone sensing in commercial products and urine samples. The XPS characterization confirmed that the optical sensing of toxic Cr(VI) is accompanied by its reduction to relatively less toxic Cr(III). These obtained results deliver a self-assured direction for the fabrication of sustainable, scalable, economically viable and highly stable dual-emissive fluorescent CDs in future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: VA acknowledges the financial grant from CONACyT Basic Sciences project A1-S-30393. We acknowledge the help provided by Dr. Valenzuela from IPN-Mexico (TEM and XPS facility).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.128830.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile synthesis of nontoxic luminescent carbon dots for detection of chromium and iron in real water sample and bio-imaging
Can. J. Chem. Eng.
